title: Influences of hydrological and agricultural conditions on selenium distribution in groundwater: A case study in Sanjiang plain, Northeast China.
author_string: Pang, Yajie, Cheng, Xuxue, He, Jin, Liu, Weipo, Niu, Xue et al.
abstract: Understanding selenium (Se) distribution mechanisms in groundwater from farmlands is crucial for ensuring water resource safety. In this study, the distribution patterns and sources of Se in the groundwater of Bawuer, Daxing, and Xinglonggang farmlands within the Naoli River Basin (Sanjiang Plain, Northeast China) were analyzed through comprehensive analyses of 154 groundwater samples and 200 soil/sediment samples from boreholes in the farmlands. Isotopic data of δD and δ<sup>18</sup>O demonstrated that precipitation and river recharge are the primary sources of groundwater. Total Se (T-Se) concentration showed an inverse correlation with hydraulic gradients but a positive association with total dissolved solids content. The statistical analysis results indicated that sulfide minerals weathering contributes 61% to selenate (SeO₄<sup>2-</sup>, 86-95% of T-Se) content, followed by the weathering of silicate and carbonate minerals and vertical leaching of Se from the vadose zone (34%). In Xinglonggang farmland, with a mean groundwater level of 2.7 m, the percentage of available Se in soils and sediments increased with depth (from 21.7% in loam in vadose zone to 43.8% in water-saturated sand); in addition, the elevated contents of SeO₄<sup>2-</sup>, humic acids, and Fe(II) in the groundwater implied an anaerobic condition, favoring the reductive decomposition of humus-associated Se from the vadose zone. These findings highlight the influences of hydrological and agricultural conditions on Se distribution, and provide critical insights for sustainable groundwater management in high-Se regions.
